text: So imagine this scenario for a second.
You have just won the biggest war of your life.
The threat to your kingdom, your home, your life is all gone.
Technically, it should be a day of massive celebration.
I mean, confetti should be falling.
Parades should be marching down the street.
But that is not what happens.
Instead, the city is completely quiet.
The soldiers, the ones who just won the war, they are sneaking in through the side gates.
They are looking at the ground.
They are acting like they are ashamed.
And from the room up above the city gate, all you can hear is a father, who is also the king, just wailing and crying.
It creates a very confusing image, doesn't it?
It is basically the definition of a paradox.
You have a military victory that feels exactly like a funeral.
And that is exactly where we are starting today.
We are looking exclusively at just one specific chapter of the Bible today.
And we are going to look at nothing else but this chapter.
It is packed with enough drama for a whole book anyway.
Now, if you are listening and you haven't read it recently, or maybe you have never read it, please don't worry.
We are going to walk through it step by step.
We will break it all down.
But our mission today isn't just to read you a story.
We are going to unpack the message.
We are going to see really complicated human emotions inside it.
This is not a fairy tale where everyone lives happily ever after.
This chapter is about the incredibly hard work of rebuilding a nation.
And rebuilding a family, really, after they have been completely shattered.
So we are going to explore how a king acts when his heart is broken,
how he handles his enemies, and the very difficult politics of forgiveness.
And to help us do this, we are using a few specific tools.
Or lenses, to look at the text today.
First, we have the literary perspective.
We are drawing some really great insights from Robert Alter's work on Biblical Narrative.
He teaches us to look at how the story is told, like paying attention to the dialogue, and also paying attention to the silence.
Because silence can speak volumes, right?
It certainly does in this story.
We are also looking at the historical and theological side of things.
We are using notes from the Logos Bible Commentary.
And also insights from Ellen G. White's commentary on these historical figures.
Those sources really help us understand what was going on inside the characters' heads.
They help explain the moral choices being made.
And just a quick note for you listening.
We are going to keep the language very simple today.
This is a complex story with a lot of politics, but we want to make it easy for anyone to follow along.
So if we hit a big complicated word, we'll just pause and explain what it means.
King David has just survived a civil war.
A war against his own son.
His son Absalom tried to kill him and take over the country.
There was a huge, violent battle in a forest.
So David is still the king.
But the cost of winning was the life of his own child.
Which brings us right into our first section.
The grieving father versus the practical general.
We are looking at the first eight verses here.
The chapter opens with King David in just absolute despair.
He keeps crying out, oh, my son Absalom, my son, my son Absalom.
Yeah, it is just the pure sound of a parent who has lost a child.
And the effect this has on his army is immediate.
Ellen White describes this moment very vividly.
She points out that the victory was basically turned into mourning or deep sadness for all the people.
That word sadness is so important here.
Think about it from the perspective of a soldier.
You just risked your life fighting in a dark, dangerous forest.
You probably expect to come home to a hero's welcome.
But instead, you have to sneak into the capital city like a thief.
The text actually says they entered the city by stealth.
Like people who are ashamed when they run away from a battle.
So the morale of the entire army just evaporated?
And this is where Joab enters the scene.
Now, to be clear, Joab is David's nephew.
He is the son of Absalom.
But more importantly, he is the general of the army.
And Joab is, well, he's not known for being a gentle guy.
Gentle is definitely not the right word for Joab.
Ellen White characterizes Joab as being rude and harsh.
He is a man of war.
He sees the world in very simple terms, black and white.
To Joab, Absalom was a bad guy.
He was a traitor who needed to die.
And David is the king who needs to sit on his throne and rule.
To Joab, it just looks like dangerous weakness.
So Joab storms into the room where the king is crying.
And he doesn't exactly offer comfort, does he?
He doesn't say, I am so sorry for your loss.
He doesn't hand him a tissue.
He launches into a verbal attack.
He tells David, you have shamed this day the faces of all your servants.
He is basically saying, you are humiliating the people who just saved your life.
He reminds David that these soldiers saved his life.
And they saved the lives of his other children and his wives.
He creates this really sharp contrast in his speech.
He accuses David of loving his enemies and hating his friends.
Joab says, I can see that if Absalom had lived and all of us had died today, you would
He is accusing the king of wishing his loyal soldiers were dead just so his traitor son
That has to hurt David to hear.
He gives the king a serious ultimatum.
Which makes it a very formal serious promise, right?
He swears that if David does not get up, go outside, and speak kindly to his servants,
there will not be a single soldier left with him by morning.
He is threatening a full mutiny.
He is telling him, stop crying and act like a king or you won't be king by tomorrow.
And Joab adds that this new disaster would be worse than all the bad things that have
happened to David since he was young.
Think about David's life for a second.
He spent years running away from the previous king, Saul.
He fought wild animals and giants.
He had a really hard life.
And Joab is saying, you think all of that was bad?
If you lose your army tonight, you will see what bad really looks like.
Now let's look at this through that literary lens we mentioned earlier from Robert Alter.
What is so interesting about David's reaction to this yelling?
